content.text: Role-play simulation assigns each agent a fixed persona — a named stakeholder with specific concerns, tone, and knowledge domain. The agents then interact through a shared transcript, each responding in character. A director node moderates the conversation, selects the next speaker, and terminates the scene. This technique generates realistic synthetic dialogue, stress-tests proposals from multiple viewpoints, and produces training data for downstream models.

attrs.alt: Role-play simulation: CTO, PM, CFO, and User agents interact through a shared transcript moderated by a Director

content.text: When to Use Role-Play Simulation
Stakeholder analysis:
simulate how a CTO, PM, and CFO would each react to a product proposal before presenting it to real stakeholders.
Red-teaming:
assign one agent as an adversary (attacker, competitor, regulator) to surface failure modes your team hasn't considered.
Synthetic data generation:
produce realistic multi-turn dialogues for fine-tuning customer service, negotiation, or interview-prep models.
Training scenario generation:
run many simulations of the same scenario with varied persona seeds to build a distribution of outcomes.
SimulationState: Persona Roster and Transcript

import operator
from typing import Annotated
from typing_extensions import TypedDict
from dataclasses import dataclass, field

@dataclass
class Persona:
    name: str
    role: str            # e.g. "CTO", "PM", "CFO"
    concerns: list[str]  # what this persona cares about
    tone: str            # "skeptical", "enthusiastic", "pragmatic"
    knowledge: str       # domain context for the system prompt

@dataclass
class SimMessage:
    persona: str
    content: str
    turn: int

class SimulationState(TypedDict):
    scenario: str                          # the simulation premise
    personas: dict[str, Persona]           # name → Persona
    transcript: Annotated[list[SimMessage], operator.add]
    turn: int
    max_turns: int
    current_speaker: str                   # which persona speaks next
    done: bool
    summary: str                           # director's final summary

def init_simulation(scenario: str, personas: list[Persona], max_turns: int = 8) -> SimulationState:
    return {
        "scenario": scenario,
        "personas": {p.name: p for p in personas},
        "transcript": [],
        "turn": 0,
        "max_turns": max_turns,
        "current_speaker": personas[0].name,
        "done": False,
        "summary": "",
    }
Persona Agent Factory
The Director Node

The director moderates the simulation. It reads the transcript, selects the next speaker, and decides when to end the scene. A rule-based director cycles through personas in order; an LLM director can dynamically choose who should respond to create more realistic dialogue.
Summary and Debrief Node
Wiring the Simulation Graph

from langgraph.graph import StateGraph, END

# Define personas
personas = [
    Persona("CTO", "Chief Technology Officer",
            ["scalability", "technical debt", "API design"],
            "skeptical", "Oversees all engineering decisions."),
    Persona("PM", "Product Manager",
            ["user value", "ship speed", "roadmap"],
            "enthusiastic", "Advocates for features users love."),
    Persona("CFO", "Chief Financial Officer",
            ["ROI", "budget", "cost efficiency"],
            "cautious", "Approves spending over $50k."),
    Persona("User", "Power User Representative",
            ["UX", "performance", "reliability"],
            "pragmatic", "Uses the product 8+ hours/day."),
]

builder = StateGraph(SimulationState)

# Add one node per persona
for persona in personas:
    builder.add_node(f"persona_{persona.name}", make_persona_agent(persona))

builder.add_node("director", llm_director)
builder.add_node("debrief", debrief_node)

builder.set_entry_point("director")

def route_after_director(state: SimulationState) -> str:
    if state["done"]:
        return "debrief"
    return f"persona_{state['current_speaker']}"

builder.add_conditional_edges("director", route_after_director,
    {f"persona_{p.name}": f"persona_{p.name}" for p in personas} | {"debrief": "debrief"}
)

# All persona nodes return to director
for persona in personas:
    builder.add_edge(f"persona_{persona.name}", "director")

builder.add_edge("debrief", END)

sim_graph = builder.compile()
Running a Simulation

scenario = """
We are proposing to add real-time collaboration (Google Docs-style cursors and co-editing)
to our project management tool. Engineering estimate: 3 months. Cost: $200k.
Each persona should respond to this pitch from their perspective.
"""

state = sim_graph.invoke(init_simulation(scenario.strip(), personas, max_turns=8))

print("=== SIMULATION TRANSCRIPT ===")
for msg in state["transcript"]:
    print(f"\n[Turn {msg.turn}] {msg.persona}:")
    print(f"  {msg.content}")

print("\n=== DEBRIEF ===")
print(state["summary"])
Generating Synthetic Training Data

import random

SCENARIO_TEMPLATES = [
    "We are considering {feature}. Engineering estimate: {weeks} weeks.",
    "A customer reported {issue}. How should we respond?",
    "Competitor X just launched {product}. What should we do?",
]

def generate_synthetic_dialogues(n: int = 10) -> list[dict]:
    """Run N simulations with varied scenarios and return transcripts."""    results = []
    features = ["dark mode", "offline support", "AI search", "mobile app", "API v2"]
    
    for i in range(n):
        feature = random.choice(features)
        weeks = random.randint(2, 12)
        scenario = f"We are considering adding {feature}. Engineering estimate: {weeks} weeks."
        
        state = sim_graph.invoke(init_simulation(scenario, personas, max_turns=6))
        
        results.append({
            "scenario": scenario,
            "transcript": [
                {"speaker": m.persona, "text": m.content, "turn": m.turn}
                for m in state["transcript"]
            ],
            "debrief": state["summary"],
        })
    
    return results

# Write training data to JSONL
import json
dialogues = generate_synthetic_dialogues(5)
with open("training_data.jsonl", "w") as f:
    for d in dialogues:
        f.write(json.dumps(d) + "\n")
print(f"Generated {len(dialogues)} training examples")
Testing Persona Consistency

import pytest
from unittest.mock import patch, MagicMock

class TestPersonaAgent:
    def _state(self, speaker="CTO", transcript=None):
        return {
            "scenario": "Pitch: add dark mode",
            "personas": {p.name: p for p in personas},
            "transcript": transcript or [],
            "turn": 0,
            "max_turns": 8,
            "current_speaker": speaker,
            "done": False,
            "summary": "",
        }
    
    @patch("persona_llm")
    def test_persona_speaks_when_selected(self, mock_llm):
        mock_llm.invoke.return_value = MagicMock(content="I need the API spec first.")
        node = make_persona_agent(personas[0])   # CTO
        state = self._state(speaker="CTO")
        result = node(state)
        assert len(result["transcript"]) == 1
        assert result["transcript"][0].persona == "CTO"
    
    @patch("persona_llm")
    def test_persona_silent_when_not_selected(self, mock_llm):
        node = make_persona_agent(personas[0])   # CTO
        state = self._state(speaker="PM")        # PM's turn
        result = node(state)
        assert result == {}  # CTO does nothing
    
    def test_director_cycles_in_order(self):
        state = self._state(speaker="CTO")
        state["turn"] = 0
        result = rule_based_director(state)
        persona_names = list(state["personas"].keys())
        assert result["current_speaker"] == persona_names[1]  # next in order
    
    def test_simulation_terminates_at_max_turns(self):
        state = self._state()
        state["turn"] = 7
        state["max_turns"] = 8
        result = rule_based_director(state)
        assert result["done"] is True
Summary
Role-play simulation assigns each agent a fixed persona (name, role, concerns, tone) and lets them interact through a shared transcript moderated by a director node.
The director selects the next speaker — either by fixed rotation (cheap) or LLM-driven selection (more natural) — and ends the scene at
max_turns
A debrief node reads the full transcript and produces a structured summary of agreements, objections, and next steps — the real deliverable from the simulation.
Use
format_transcript(last_n=6)
to limit context size — each agent does not need the full history, only the recent turns relevant to their response.
For synthetic data generation, vary the scenario seed across runs and collect transcripts in JSONL format for downstream fine-tuning.
